stories.headline: Denver City Council delays vote on Axon license plate reader contract amid privacy concerns
stories.summary:
Denver City Council postponed a vote Monday on a proposed one-year contract with Axon to replace the city's expiring Flock license plate reader agreement, leaving the city days away from losing its current automated license plate recognition capability. Several council members said they felt rushed and that questions about data storage and sharing had not been fully resolved. Dozens of residents spoke against the technology during public comment, with critics warning of surveillance overreach and comparisons to an "AI-powered police state."

The proposed Axon contract would cost $150,000 and allow installation of 50 license plate readers across Denver. City officials said Denver would own its own data, that Axon does not pool or sell data, and that a 21-day retention policy — shorter than Flock's 30-day window — would apply. Access would be limited to Denver Safety personnel.

Mayor Mike Johnston's director of policy told council that since summer 2024, Flock cameras helped Denver police recover 446 stolen vehicles, make 528 arrests, and seize 63 firearms. He also said license plate reader data was used in more than 40% of the city's 2025 homicide investigations. Supporters of the new contract argued the technology is a critical force multiplier for law enforcement.

stories.headline: Denver Water board set to vote Wednesday on Stage I restrictions as snowpack hits record lows
stories.summary:
The Denver Board of Water Commissioners is scheduled to vote Wednesday, March 25, on proposed Stage I water restrictions that would affect approximately 1.5 million residents across Denver, Arvada, Littleton, Lakewood, and other served communities. If approved, the restrictions would be the first at that level since 2013, according to the utility. Denver Water said snowpack in its collection areas is at or near record lows, with Colorado River snowpack ranked the worst on record and South Platte River snowpack also at its worst in the agency's decades of data.

Under Stage I restrictions, lawn watering would be limited to two days per week on a published schedule, with no grass irrigation allowed between 10 a.m. and 6 p.m. Trees, shrubs, perennials, and vegetables could be watered by hand any day outside those hours. Restaurants would be required to serve water only on request, and hotels would face limits on how often linens are laundered.

Other metro-area water providers are also moving toward restrictions. Thornton and Erie are already under water use limits. Aurora is weighing Stage I restrictions that could begin April 7, and Brighton is expected to decide on restrictions around May 1. Erie has declared a Water Supply Shortage Response Level 4 Emergency, with all residential sprinklers ordered off through the end of March.

stories.headline: Victim identified in Longmont teen stepbrother shooting; first-degree murder charge filed
stories.summary:
The 16-year-old boy fatally shot by his stepbrother in a Longmont apartment on March 18 has been identified as Blaiker Yepez Mambel, according to the Boulder County Coroner's Office. Blaiker was found with a gunshot wound in a bedroom at a Cloverbasin Village apartment on Redmond Drive and was pronounced dead at the scene. His minor stepbrother, whose name has not been released, called police after the shooting and told dispatchers he had killed someone.

The stepbrother is facing a first-degree murder charge, according to the Boulder County District Attorney's Office. Responding officers found the stepbrother outside the apartment holding a Glock handgun, which he dropped upon police commands. A hearing will be held to determine whether the case remains in juvenile court or proceeds with the stepbrother tried as an adult.

Family members told police that Blaiker and his stepbrother had a troubled relationship and had engaged in physical fights before. Relatives said they were unaware of any firearms in the home and did not know how the stepbrother obtained the weapon.

stories.headline: One dead, two injured in I-225 rideshare rollover crash; two fled scene
stories.summary:
Aurora police are searching for two people who fled the scene of a fatal rollover crash on Interstate 225 near East Iliff Avenue early Saturday morning. A rideshare driver operating a Toyota Corolla heading northbound with two passengers rear-ended a Chevy Silverado around 2:15 a.m., causing the Toyota to roll before coming to rest on the highway shoulder. Speed is believed to have been a factor in the crash.

One of the backseat passengers, a man whose identity is pending release by the Arapahoe County coroner's office, was killed. The driver and the second passenger were transported to a hospital with serious injuries. Both occupants of the Silverado exited the vehicle and ran from the scene after the collision.

Aurora police are asking anyone with information about the identities of the two people who fled to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Model rocket ignites 7-acre Broomfield grass fire during active fire ban; person cited
stories.summary:
A grass fire that prompted pre-evacuation warnings for part of the Wildgrass neighborhood in Broomfield on Monday afternoon was accidentally sparked by someone launching model rockets at Remote Control Airfield Park, northwest of 144th Avenue and Lowell Boulevard. Wind caused the fire to grow to approximately 7 acres before North Metro Fire Rescue crews contained it around 3:35 p.m. No injuries were reported.

Model rockets are prohibited under the Level 2 fire ban currently in effect across the district. Broomfield police cited the individual responsible for violating open burning restrictions during a fire ban. Fire officials noted that dry conditions made the situation escalate quickly.

"With current conditions, it doesn't take much to start a wildfire. Please follow all fire restrictions and use extreme caution outdoors," North Metro Fire Rescue stated in a post following the incident.

stories.headline: Boring Co. passes on Denver's Ball Arena tunnel proposal, selects three other U.S. cities
stories.summary:
Elon Musk's Boring Company has declined to move forward with a proposed pedestrian tunnel at Denver's Ball Arena, choosing instead to pursue projects in Baltimore, Dallas, and New Orleans as part of its national "Tunnel Vision Challenge" competition. Denver had been named one of 16 finalists for the initiative, which centers on a concept for a one-mile underground loop tunnel. The proposed Denver project would have connected Ball Arena to the neighboring Elitch Gardens amusement park, both owned by Kroenke Sports and Entertainment.

The Boring Company's decision means Denver will not be getting a pedestrian tunnel from the venture-backed tunneling firm, at least under this program. The Tunnel Vision Challenge was designed to identify cities where the company would build a demonstration project.

The news comes as Denver continues to weigh significant infrastructure investments around Ball Arena, including the broader Burnham Yards stadium development project. No details were provided on whether KSE or city officials plan to pursue alternative connectivity solutions between the two entertainment venues.

stories.headline: DIA's $2.1 billion terminal overhaul keeps security wait times under 35 minutes during spring break surge
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport's investment in redesigned security checkpoints has helped keep wait times far below those at other major U.S. airports during the simultaneous pressures of a spring break travel surge and the ongoing partial federal government shutdown. Average wait times at DIA over the past two weeks were 12 minutes, with a maximum of 35 minutes, according to airport records. By contrast, travelers at numerous other airports have faced hours-long TSA lines.

As part of a $2.1 billion main terminal overhaul, DIA installed 34 AI-powered security lanes that allow passengers to keep laptops in bags and use facial recognition technology, replacing 42 older-style lanes. The airport donated $60 million worth of checkpoint equipment to TSA along with manufacturer warranty and support services. Each lane can process 180 to 240 passengers per hour.

TSA workers nationwide have been required to work without pay for more than a month due to the shutdown that began January 31, contributing to staffing shortages at other airports. DIA launched a donations campaign that collected more than 950 grocery and gas gift cards for its TSA officers. Airport officials said TSA workers have continued to show up to work at DIA despite the lack of pay.

stories.headline: Aurora council advances updated MOU with ICE detention facility one year after detainee escapes
stories.summary:
Aurora City Council members approved moving forward Monday with a revised memorandum of understanding between the Aurora Police Department and the GEO Group's Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facility in the city. The updated agreement, which will come before the council again for a formal vote, establishes clearer guidelines for police response when detainees escape, including defined communication protocols, command structure, and a legal framework. The update was prompted by a 2025 incident in which two detainees escaped during a power outage and accusations were exchanged between ICE and Aurora police over who was at fault.

Aurora Police Chief Todd Chamberlain said ICE claimed local authorities were notified immediately and declined to assist, while Aurora police maintain the 911 call did not come until 2:30 a.m. — four to five hours after the men escaped. "There was a lot of conversation about Aurora police dropping the ball. Well, we didn't drop the ball," Chamberlain told council members.

Chamberlain emphasized the new MOU does not expand APD's authority or deputize officers as immigration enforcement agents. Escaping from the ICE facility is a criminal offense, Deputy Chief Mark Hildebrand noted, which is why police involvement centers on criminal law rather than immigration enforcement.

stories.headline: Shannon Bird's voting record on immigration, taxes comes under scrutiny in Colorado's 8th Congressional District primary
stories.summary:
Former state Rep. Shannon Bird, who is running to unseat Republican U.S. Rep. Gabe Evans in Colorado's competitive 8th Congressional District, is facing criticism in the Democratic primary over a series of votes in which she broke with her party. Bird voted against bills to further limit state and local government cooperation with federal immigration authorities, opposed a measure to end certain business tax breaks in favor of lower-income tax benefits, and proposed a more lenient alternative to a short-term rental property tax bill. She also voted against a rent control measure backed by most House Democrats.

Bird's primary opponents, led by state Rep. Manny Rutinel of Commerce City, have seized on those votes and her leadership of a nonprofit caucus for moderate Democrats that does not publicly disclose its donors and has reported ties to conservative interests. Some Democratic voters say they want a fighter who will take harder-line positions, while others believe Bird's more centrist approach is better suited to winning in a district that includes conservative Weld County and Greeley.

Bird has defended her record as pragmatic problem-solving, saying she votes with the majority of Democrats on most issues. "When people come to the table with goodwill intent to solve problems, I'll work with them," she said. The 8th District, which Bird represented parts of in the state legislature, is considered one of Colorado's most competitive swing districts heading into the November general election.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature passes resolution urging federal action on tribal water rights as funding freeze stalls projects
stories.summary:
The Colorado House of Representatives unanimously passed a nonbinding resolution Friday urging federal agencies to fulfill water-related obligations to the Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute Indian tribes, including releasing frozen funding for tribal water infrastructure projects. The measure, which now awaits consideration in the Senate, was introduced on Ute Day at the Capitol and calls on the federal government to address a range of tribal water priorities, from repairing deteriorating irrigation systems to improving reservoir access at Lake Nighthorse near Durango.

Among the projects highlighted is a water line serving the Ute Mountain Ute Tribe's reservation at Towaoc, for which federal funding was awarded but frozen by the Trump administration. The Southern Ute Indian Tribe has also been waiting for federal assistance to overhaul an irrigation system dating to the late 1800s, of which only about 15% of the 175 miles of canals are in good condition. Estimates put full repairs at up to $126 million; about $20 million in Bureau of Reclamation grants has been held up by the administration's freeze on Inflation Reduction Act spending.

Senate Minority Leader Cleave Simpson, a Republican whose district includes both tribal reservations, co-sponsored the resolution and acknowledged its limitations. "I guess it's wishful thinking, but that's what we can do at this level," he said, expressing hope that federal agencies would pay closer attention to the tribes' long-pending needs.

stories.headline: Colorado's record-low snowpack forces early closures at ski resorts; Vail holds April 19 date despite deteriorating conditions
stories.summary:
Colorado ski resorts are grappling with historically low snowpack and unusually rapid melt conditions, with some areas already shutting down and others struggling to justify remaining open through their planned closing dates. At Vail Mountain, just 42% of 277 trails are currently open, with multiple back bowls closed and the resort's website warning of "variable conditions" and "unmarked obstacles." The current base depth of 40 inches is 36% of normal.

Vail has held firm to its official closing date of April 19 despite pressure from local business owners and longtime ski industry observers who say conditions are deteriorating by the day. Buzz Schleper, who has operated a ski shop in Vail Village for more than four decades, predicted the resort will be forced to close the bottom of the mountain within a week and that Vail will be a "ghost town" after Easter weekend on April 5.

The early-season melt reflects the broader drought gripping Colorado, with Denver Water reporting record-low snowpack in its watershed collection areas as of late March. High temperatures in Denver are expected to reach into the 80s in the coming weeks, further accelerating snowmelt at elevation and driving demand for water restrictions across the metro area.

stories.headline: Colorado Joint Budget Committee rejects transparency measure for wolf reintroduction spending
stories.summary:
Colorado's Joint Budget Committee voted Monday against a staff recommendation to create a separate budget line item for Colorado Parks and Wildlife's wolf reintroduction spending, opting instead to request additional information on the agency's progress implementing preventive measures tied to the wolf program. JBC staff had proposed the dedicated line item to clearly show how much CPW spends acquiring wolves, noting that current costs are folded into a broader wildlife operations line. CPW opposed the change, arguing its annual wolf report provides sufficient transparency — though the most recent report did not disclose the cost of acquiring 15 wolves from British Columbia in January 2025.

The decision comes as CPW seeks $450,000 in general funds for 2026-27 — double its 2025 wolf spending — to acquire additional wolves, even though the agency has not identified where those animals would come from. Colorado faces a projected $1.5 billion shortfall in its 2026-27 general fund budget. In a special session last August, lawmakers stripped CPW of $264,268 in wolf acquisition funds and placed restrictions on further spending until state-funded preventive measures are implemented "to the highest degree possible."

Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer, R-Brighton, argued that greater transparency would help lawmakers verify whether CPW has met requirements set by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and fulfilled the conditions attached to last year's special session restrictions. Rep. Kyle Brown, D-Louisville, was skeptical of adding the line item, questioning what lawmakers would gain from the change.

weather.notable: About 33 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I don't watch the news. I just wait to hear about stuff. If it's big enough, it'll get to me.

topStory.headline: Democrat Flips Florida House Seat in District Covering Trump's Mar-a-Lago
topStory.summary:
Democrat Emily Gregory won a special election Tuesday for Florida's 87th state House district, which encompasses President Donald Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, defeating Trump-endorsed Republican Jon Maples by roughly two percentage points. The Associated Press projected Gregory the winner with all precincts reporting, marking a significant reversal in a district where a Republican won by 19 percentage points in 2024 and Trump himself carried by approximately 11 points that same year.

Gregory, a public health expert and small business owner running for office for the first time, centered her campaign on rising costs and promised to prioritize that issue in office. Maples, a financial adviser and former Lake Clarke Shores council member who described himself as an 'America-First patriot,' touted his business experience and pledged to cut taxes. Trump had endorsed Maples on Truth Social, urging supporters to turn out, and cast his own ballot in the race by mail — despite the president's longstanding public criticism of mail-in voting as fraudulent.

The victory is the 10th GOP-held state legislative seat Democrats have flipped since Trump began his second term in January 2025. Republicans have not flipped any Democratic state legislative seats during the same period. Democratic Legislative Campaign Committee president Heather Williams said state Democrats have flipped 29 districts since Trump's election, while Florida Democratic Party chair Nikki Fried declared the result proof that 'Democrats can run and win anywhere — including Donald Trump's backyard.'

The win carries broader significance as both parties look toward the November 2026 midterm elections, in which control of the U.S. House, a third of the Senate, and numerous governorships and state legislatures will be on the ballot. Trump's approval rating currently sits in the high 30s to low 40s in most public polling, and Americans have expressed broad skepticism toward the ongoing U.S. war with Iran, which has dominated coverage of the administration for the past month. Democratic National Committee chair Ken Martin said voters had 'sent a message,' adding: 'If Democrats can win in Trump's backyard, we sure as hell can win anywhere across the country.'
